nodes.hook: Brahma and the demigods petition Vishnu to relieve Earth; Vasudeva weds Devaki; Kamsa hears the prophecy.
Kamsa kills six newborns; the demigods praise Krishna already present in Devaki's womb.
At midnight on Janmashtami, Krishna appears in four-armed form; Vasudeva carries Him across the Yamuna to Gokula.
Yogamaya slips from Kamsa's grasp and reveals his killer is already born elsewhere.
Nanda celebrates the birth-festival in Gokula and meets Vasudeva while paying taxes in Mathura.
The poison-breasted demoness Putana is killed at infant Krishna's nursing.
Baby Krishna kicks over the cart-demon Shakatasura and strangles the whirlwind-demon Trinavarta.
Garga Muni names Krishna and Balarama; Yashoda sees the entire cosmos inside Krishna's mouth.
Yashoda binds the butter-thief to a mortar — the Damodara lila.
Krishna drags the mortar between two trees, liberating Kuvera's cursed sons.
The cowherds move to Vrindavana; Krishna kills the calf-demon Vatsasura and the crane-demon Bakasura.
Krishna and the boys are swallowed by the python-demon Aghasura, who dies from within.
Brahma steals the boys and calves; Krishna expands Himself as every one of them for a year.
Humbled Brahma offers his celebrated prayers to the cowherd boy.
Balarama slays the donkey-demon Dhenuka in the Talavana grove.
Krishna leaps into the Yamuna and dances on the hoods of the serpent Kaliya.
Kaliya's history is told; Krishna swallows a raging forest fire to save the cowherds.
In the rainy-season games, Balarama destroys the demon Pralamba.
Krishna again swallows a forest fire that engulfs the sleeping cowherds.
The rainy season and autumn in Vrindavana are described in poetic detail.
The gopis glorify the song of Krishna's flute that steals their hearts.
Krishna steals the bathing gopis' garments, teaching them surrender.
The wives of ritualistic brahmins feed Krishna and are blessed; their husbands repent.
On Krishna's advice, the Vrajavasis worship Govardhana Hill instead of Indra.
Enraged Indra floods Vraja; Krishna lifts Govardhana as an umbrella for seven days.
The elders marvel at Krishna's deeds; Nanda recalls Garga's prophecies.
Indra and mother Surabhi crown Krishna as "Govinda."
Krishna rescues Nanda from Varuna's realm and shows the cowherds His spiritual abode.
On a full-moon autumn night, Krishna's flute calls the gopis to the rasa dance.
Abandoned by Krishna mid-dance, the gopis search the forest singing of Him.
The gopis sing the celebrated Gopi-gita in separation.
Krishna returns to the gopis and explains why He had hidden.
The rasa dance unfolds — Krishna multiplies Himself between every two gopis.
A Yaksha kidnaps a gopi; Krishna slays him (Shankhachuda) and rescues Nanda from a serpent.
The gopis sing of Krishna throughout day and night as He grazes the cows.
The bull-demon Aristasura attacks Vraja and is slain.
Krishna kills the horse-demon Keshi and Vyomasura the cave-demon.
Sent by Kamsa, Akrura arrives in Vrindavana to fetch Krishna and Balarama.
The brothers depart with Akrura; the gopis collapse in grief.
Bathing in the Yamuna, Akrura beholds Krishna's Vaikuntha form and offers prayers.
Entering Mathura, Krishna blesses Trivakra and the garland-maker Sudama.
Krishna straightens Trivakra and breaks Kamsa's sacrificial bow.
The brothers kill the elephant Kuvalayapida guarding Kamsa's arena.
In the arena, Krishna dispatches Chanura and Mushtika and drags Kamsa from his throne — slain at last.
After freeing Devaki and Vasudeva, Krishna studies under Sandipani and brings back his guru's son from Yamaloka.
Krishna sends Uddhava to Vrindavana to console Nanda, Yashoda, and the gopis.
Uddhava delivers Krishna's message; a bee becomes the gopis' audience in the Bhramara-gita.
Back in Mathura, Krishna visits Trivakra and Akrura and plans the next moves.
Krishna sends Akrura to Hastinapura to learn of the Pandavas' welfare.
Attacked seventeen times by Jarasandha, Krishna builds the sea-fortress Dwarka and shifts the Yadavas.
The yavana king Kalayavana pursues Krishna into a cave; Mucukunda's glance burns him to ash.
Princess Rukmini sends a secret letter pleading to be rescued from marriage to Shishupala.
Krishna abducts Rukmini from her wedding procession and defeats the assembled kings.
Balarama spares Rukmini's brother Rukmi but disfigures him; Krishna weds Rukmini in Dwarka.
Pradyumna is born, kidnapped by Shambara, returns to slay him and marries Mayavati.
Satrajit's Syamantaka jewel is lost; Krishna recovers it from Jambavan after a long combat.
Satrajit is murdered; Krishna recovers the jewel and clears His name.
Krishna marries Kalindi, Mitravinda, Satya, Bhadra, and Lakshmana — five more queens.
Krishna kills Narakasura, frees 16,100 captive princesses, and accepts them as wives.
In the inner chambers, Krishna playfully teases Rukmini until she faints in love.
Krishna's many sons and Rukmi's descendants are described.
Aniruddha is captured by Banasura after a secret love-affair with princess Usha.
Krishna defeats Banasura — Shiva himself yields — and frees Aniruddha.
King Nriga, cursed to be a lizard for a brahmana's cow, is liberated by Krishna's touch.
Balarama visits Vrindavana, sports with the gopis, and drags the Yamuna with His plough.
The imposter Paundraka, who claims to be Vasudeva, is killed along with his Kashi ally.
Balarama slays the gorilla-demon Dvivida who terrorized Anarta.
Samba abducts Duryodhana's daughter; Balarama drags Hastinapura with His plough until peace is made.
Narada visits each of Krishna's 16,108 palaces and finds Him present in every one.
Krishna's daily royal duties; messengers arrive from kings imprisoned by Jarasandha.
On Narada's counsel, Krishna travels to Indraprastha to assist Yudhishthira's Rajasuya.
Krishna, Bhima, and Arjuna confront Jarasandha; Bhima kills him in single combat.
The 20,800 freed kings glorify Krishna and return to their kingdoms.
At the Rajasuya, Krishna receives the first worship; Shishupala insults Him and is beheaded.
The completed Rajasuya leaves Duryodhana humiliated in the illusory palace of Maya.
Shalva attacks Dwarka with his magical airship Saubha.
Krishna pursues Shalva, shatters the Saubha, and kills him.
Shishupala's brother Dantavakra is slain; Vidura's pilgrimage is mentioned.
Balarama tours the holy places during the Kurukshetra war, avoiding combat.
The poor brahmana Sudama, Krishna's classmate, visits Dwarka with a humble offering of flat rice.
Krishna welcomes Sudama royally; Sudama returns home to find his hut transformed into a palace.
During a solar eclipse, all the Yadavas meet the Vrajavasis at Kurukshetra — the long-awaited reunion.
The queens of Krishna gather with Draupadi and share how each was won.
Sages assemble at Kurukshetra; Krishna instructs Nanda and the Vrajavasis before they part.
Krishna instructs Vasudeva on the Supreme Truth and recovers Devaki's six sons killed by Kamsa.
Arjuna abducts Subhadra with Krishna's blessing; the sage Shrutadeva and King Bahulashva are honoured.
The personified Vedas offer profound prayers describing the Absolute Truth.
The demon Vrikasura tries to behead Shiva; Vishnu tricks him into destroying himself.
After a dispute among the gods, Krishna and Arjuna travel beyond material creation to recover a brahmana's lost sons.
Sukadeva summarises Krishna's pastimes, wives, sons — and the supreme glories of the Lord.
